So, I’m in the middle of a heavy semester, trying to fit this ML course into short evening study sessions, and I barely passed after I wrestled with it most of the term. The lectures are useful once you’re able to sit through them, but they’re mostly dry and hard to stay engaged with after a long day. I liked that the materials were solid and the core ideas were laid out clearly enough to follow along if I forced myself to take notes, but the whole tool setup took more time than the payoff, which was frustrating when you’re juggling other classes. Most of the work was solo, with little peer dependency, so I spent more time chasing answers in forums than actually collaborating, which felt oddly isolating. The staff weren’t as responsive as I’d hoped, and it seemed like students helped each other more than the instructors or TAs did, which made the support feel adrift. A concrete moment that sticks: I spent an entire evening wrestling with configuring the experiment runner, only to realize the grading quirks skewed the deadline by a day, which added unnecessary stress. I’d probably avoid taking it again, even though it did push me to figure things out, but I’m glad I learned something workable from the grind.

Taking Computer Networks this term basically became my main focus, and I leaned on recordings and notes a lot because the live lectures didn’t stick for me, even though the slides were serviceable. I appreciated the practical bite in the project where we mapped a small network and performance measurements, but the grading comments dragged a bit after the deadline, which left the mid-semester crunch feeling even rougher.

Midway through the heavy stretch of this semester, I’m grappling with whether the material really clicked, but I’ll admit the peer discussion has saved me more than the official readings. Dr. Singh delivers clear lectures with solid pacing, and the way we bounce ideas in small groups often clarifies things the slides gloss over, which helps my understanding even when I’m unsure about the math-heavy parts. The course fills in prerequisite gaps better than I expected, and I ended up relying on quick forum threads and the TAs during office hours more than the staff did, especially when a concept like process scheduling didn’t click right away. I’d say collaboration structure matters here; chatting with a partner helped more than slogging through the forum posts alone. I’m on the fence about the final grade; a C feels like it could reflect my grasp of the core ideas, not just the messy week-to-week work, and I’m still unsure if I truly understand some of the depth. There’s a real upside, though: practical application shows up in how we discuss real OS quirks during peer discussions, which I actually found useful for applying concepts to projects.

Midway through the semester in Machine Learning, I’m feeling mixed but mostly stuck in the middle of a heavy crunch. The quick feedback on scores comes through fast, but the explanations feel pretty thin, which makes it hard to tell if a C actually means I’m missing the point or just not getting the phrasing right yet. I’m new to the discipline, so I’m still learning the basics, and the few assessments that actually matter—there aren’t many, but they loom large—have me scrambling to catch up around deadlines. I do better when I have a study group, and this course kind of shines there; chatting through a confusing concept helps more than the lecture slides, which feel a bit boilerplate. The tooling setup adds friction—setting up the environment and chasing library versions ate into learning time, and I’m not sure the payoff matches the effort. Compared to another course I took this term, this one dragged a bit during the mid–semester crunch, even though the exam felt fair and the material aligned with what I’m trying to grasp, if not mastered yet. Overall, I’m still figuring out whether I’m getting the right signal from my work.

I took Machine Learning with Prof. Cohen mainly because I needed it for a specialization, and I wasn’t sure what I’d signed up for—proofs make sense to me, but systems work tends to stress me out, so I came in feeling cautiously confident yet wary of the tooling maze. The pacing dragged in the middle of the term, especially when deadlines kept piling up and the tooling hiccups kept throwing me off; the platform would glitch or the repo wouldn’t sync right when I was juggling another hard class, and it felt like I was scrambling to regather momentum. I did feel like I learned a lot, especially by the late-semester turning point when we finally got a clearer sense of the core ideas, but the overall experience left me with a constant sense of drift—the lectures are recorded and useful, but they read as dry and rarely sparked much engagement, which didn’t help when attendance faded. The requirements skew toward report-heavy work and lengthy writeups, which is fine for me since I can follow proofs, but the feedback came sparsely until it was basically too late to fix anything meaningful. I’d recommend it only if you’re prepared to push through the rough patches with solid time management and a tolerance for late feedback.
